0: Substitution of corn silage with sorghum silages in lactating cow diets: In vivo methane emission and global warming potential of milk production

Stefania Colombini, Maddalena Zucali, Luca Rapetti, G. Matteo Crovetto, Anna Sandrucci Luciana Bava Università degli Studi di Milano, Dipartimento Scienze Agrarie e Ambientali, via Celoria 2, 20133 Milano, Italy

A R T INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 13 August 2014 Received in revised form 5 February 2015 Accepted 15 February 2015 Available online 21 March 2015 Keywords: Milk Sorghum silage Methane Global warming potential LCA|"A study was conducted to determine whether sorghum silage can be a valid alternative to corn silage in lactating cow rations in terms of enteric CH4 production and global warming potential (GWP) per milk unit. Diets containing corn (CS), whole plant grain sorghum (WPGS) or forage sorghum (FS) silages were fed to 6 primiparous Italian Friesian cows in a replicated 3 X 3 Latin square design. Diets were balanced to have 36.0 and 26.0% DM of NDF and starch, respectively. In each period cows were placed in individual respiration chambers to register CH4 production. The GWP of milk production in the three different forage systems was evaluated through a ""cradle to farm-gate"" Life Cycle Assessment. All the emissions related to on-farm activities (forage production, fuel and electricity consumptions, manure and livestock management), off-farm activities (production of fertilizers, pesticides, bedding materials, purchased forages, concentrate feed, replacement animals, electricity, fuel) and transportation were considered. The functional unit was 1 kg FPCM. Enteric emission of CH4 from cows was obtained with an in vivo experiment or predicted using an equation based on gross energy intake and on dietary NDF and ether extract. Dry matter intake (kg/day) tended to be higher for CS and WPGS (20.0 for both diets) than FS (18.2) (P = 0.07). Milk yield was 23.6, 24.6 and 25.4 kg/day for FS, WPGS and CS, respectively (P = 0.05 between CS and FS). On average, CH4 from enteric fermentation and manure storage was the major contributor (45.4%) to GHG emission of milk production and 71.1% of the CH4 was from enteric losses. Predicted CH4 emission was slightly lower than the emission measured from in vivo trials (323 VS 340 g/day per cow on average). In vivo CH4 production was not different among diets but intake energy lost as CH4 was higher (P = 0.04) for FS (5.8%) in comparison with CS and WPGS (5.1 and 5.2% for CS and WPGS, respectively). Contribution of on-farm crop production to GWP was lower for sorghum scenarios, particularly FS, due to the reduced use of water and fertilizers. On the contrary purchased concentrate feed showed a great load on GWP (30%), especially in the FS scenario, because of the greater amount of corn meal needed to compensate for the low starch content of the sorghum silages. In conclusion the CS forage system gave higher milk production, lower CH4 energy loss and lower GWP per kg FPCM than the sorghum forage systems. © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved."
1: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions play an important role in in- creasing global temperature: up to 13.5% of all anthropogenic GHG emissions derive in particular from agricultural systems (IPCC, 2007). In a study of Pulina et al. (2011), on the Italian livestock sector, the contribution of animal husbandry to total GHG production was es- timated at 3.2% although there is still uncertainty about the real impact of animal husbandry on GHG (Pirondini et al., 2011). About

44% of GHG emissions of the livestock sector are in the form of CH4 while the remaining part is almost equally shared between N20 (29%) and CO2 (27%) (Gerber et al., 2013). According to Guerci et al. (2013a) the main components of global warming potential (GWP) of cow milk production in intensive systems are: emissions from barns, manure storage and handling (together 50.1% of the total GHG) and emissions for production and transportation of purchased concen- trate feeds (together 21.2% of the total GHG). Diet manipulation is the most direct method of lowering CH4 emissions from ruminants (Beauchemin et al., 2008). In general enteric CH4 emissions are predicted from the chemical analysis of the diet through several equations (Ellis et al., 2007; Hristov et al., 2013; Moraes et al., 2014); however, these equations do not seem

accurate and appropriate for all feeding situations (Hindrichsen et al., 2005). For example, IPCC (2006a) published guidelines are used for official estimates of CH4 emission, but Kebreab et al. (2008) showed that CH4 emissions inventories are more accurately estimated through diet-specific mechanistic models. Similarly, Chagunda et al. (2010) stated that, in order to mitigate CH4 emissions in a way ac- ceptable for both the environment and animal welfare, it is important to quantify the effects of different diets on methane emissions. For example, replacing corn silage with grass silage increases CH4 emis- sion from dairy cows (Doreau et al., 2012; Mills et al., 2001) mainly as a consequence of the different starch and fiber contents of the diets. Other results suggest that a remarkable difference in dietary starch concentration is required to alter ruminal methanogenesis (Hassanat et al., 2013).

Overall, current models may give inaccurate estimates of CH4 emission levels because they cannot predict the wide range in enteric CH4 emissions as affected by dry matter intake (DMI) and type of diet. Additional studies are needed to better define the interac- tions between DMI, quality and intake of forages and CH4 emissions.

Production of feed for livestock is another factor that contrib- utes to GHG emission directly because of fossil fuel inputs (cultivation, transportation and feed processing) and indirectly through land-cover change both for grazing and feed cultivation (Vermeulen et al., 2012). Steinfeld et al. (2006) estimated that more than half of the total energy consumed in livestock production is used for feed production and that about 20% of the 80 million tonnes of nitrogen fertilizers produced annually are used to produce live- stock feed. Five of the most widely grown crops in the world which can be used as animal feed are wheat, corn, soybean, barley and sorghum (FAO, 2012). Among these crops, a modeling study (Lobell and Field, 2007) showed that for wheat, corn and barley there is a clear negative response of global yields to increased temperatures (since 1980s) while for sorghum, the effect is less pronounced.

Sorghum is increasing in popularity as a silage crop, primarily because it requires less water and agronomic inputs than corn (Marsalis et al., 2010). Considering that competition for depleting resources (for example water, fossil fuel, etc.) is now widely seen as the foremost challenge of our time (Vermeulen et al., 2012), sorghum can be a valuable alternative low-input crop for dairy cattle feeding. Northern Italy dairy farming systems are characterized by a high number of cows per hectare (Guerci et al., 2013b); hence, the use of crops with high DM yield and net energy is mandatory. Amodeo (2007) reported average yields of 18.8 and 13.2 t DM/ha for corn and sorghum forage silages in the Po plain and sorghum ranked second in yield among all tested forages. Colombo et al. (2007) reported a lower average net energy value (4.91 MJ NE1/kg DM) for sorghum forage varieties harvested in the North of Italy as compared to the average value (5.51 MJ NE1/kg DM) reported by Spanghero et al. (2009) for corn silage harvested in the same area. However, dairy cattle fed sorghum silage gave similar milk pro- duction as corn silage (Aydin et al., 1999; Grant et al., 1995; Oliver et al., 2004).

The objectives of our study were: (1) to determine the effects of replacing corn silage with grain sorghum and forage sorghum silages in dairy cow diets on in vivo enteric CH4 production mea- sured in respiration chambers; (2) to compare the global warming potential of milk production in three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages, using pre- dicted or in vivo measured enteric methane emissions.

The objectives of our study were: (1) to determine the effects of replacing corn silage with grain sorghum and forage sorghum silages in dairy cow diets on in vivo enteric CH4 production mea- sured in respiration chambers; (2) to compare the global warming potential of milk production in three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages, using pre- dicted or in vivo measured enteric methane emissions.

12: Three air-flow controlled chambers were used for measuring CH4 production. The chambers measured 3.6 m length X 2.4 m

width X 2.3 m height. When the chamber door was closed, air entered the chamber through a ventilation duct and exited through an exhaust. Air temperature within the chamber was maintained at 22 + 1 °C. Air flow into and out of the chamber was measured using an inline mass flowmeter (Ph 20, Sacofgas, Città di Castello, Perugia, Italy). Methane concentration was continuously measured at the air entrance and exhaust ducts using a CH4 analyzer (Uras 4, Hartmann & Braunn, ABB spa - Process Automation Division, Sesto San Giovanni, Italy). The amounts of CH4 entering and leaving the chamber were calculated by multiplying the concentration of CH4 by the airflow at the en- trance and exhaust. Cows were accustomed to the environmental chambers before the beginning of the experiment. Methane was re- corded every seven minutes over a period of 7 consecutive days and air fluxes were averaged to derive 24-h CH4 emissions.

Methane loss as percentage of gross energy intake was calcu- lated by dividing the energy of methane daily produced (assuming that 1 g of methane has an energy content of 55.5 kJ) for the GE intake. Dietary GE was determined using an adiabatic bomb calo- rimeter (IKA 4000; IKA Werke GmbH & Co. KG, Staufen, Germany).

14: <header>2.3. Life cycle assessment of milk production in three forage systems </header>

This study considered three different scenarios based on double- cropping systems with different crop rotations: corn for silage-Italian ryegrass; whole plant grain sorghum-Italian ryegrass; forage sorghum-Italian ryegrass.

The global warming potential of milk production in the three scenarios was evaluated with a "cradle to farm-gate" life cycle assessment (LCA) approach. The functional unit was 1 kg Fat and Protein Corrected Milk (FPCM) leaving the farm gate calculated with the equation proposed by Gerber et al. (2010).
The system boundaries included all the on-farm plus the off- farm activities, linked to production of external inputs, as showed in Fig. 1.

For the inventory phase a model of an intensive dairy farm located in the North of Italy was considered. On this farm, 186 dairy cows were reared in cubicle housing with straw bedding. The herd in- cluded 18 dry cows, 116 heifers between 6 and 24 month of ages and 20 female calves. The length of calving-to-conception interval was on average 175 days and the average length of lactation was 323 + 30 days. The total farm land was 48 ha; 12 ha were used for permanent grassland, with a production of 12.1 t DM/ha, while 36 ha were allocated for the three double-cropping systems according to

the three scenarios (Italian ryegrass-corn for silage; Italian ryegrass- sorghum for grain silage and Italian ryegrass-sorghum for forage silage). Agronomic data of the three double-cropping systems were obtained from parcels (see section 2.1). Crop DM yields per hectare were: 15.4 t DM/ha for corn for silage; 12.5 t DM/ha for sorghum for grain silage; 12.9 t DM/ha for sorghum for forage silage and 7.4 t DM/ha for Italian ryegrass.

The average milk production of lactating cows during the ex- periment in the respiration chambers was: 25.4 kg/day for CS diet; 24.6 kg/day for WPGS diet and 23.6 kg/day for FS diet. These values were assumed as average milk yields in the three scenarios.

All emissions related to on-farm activities (forage production, fuel and electricity consumption, manure and livestock manage- ment) and off-farm activities (production of fertilizers, pesticides, electricity, fuel, bedding materials, purchased forages and concen- trate feed - from crop growing to feed factory processing - and rearing of replacement animals) were taken into account. Trans- portation associated with the production chain of purchased feed (both concentrate feed and roughages) and bedding materials was included. The details of inventory data of off-farm processes were reported by Bava et al. (2014). The related emission of off-farm ac- tivities were calculated using the software Simapro 7.3.3 (PRé Consultants, 2011). Carbon dioxide emission from livestock respi- ration was not considered (Steinfeld et al., 2006) as well as potential variation in soil carbon stocks.

Regarding on-farm emission, enteric production of CH4 from COWS was obtained in two ways: from the in vivo experiment in respi- ration chambers and using the equations proposed by Moraes et al. (2014) based on gross energy intake, NDF and ether extract of the ration for lactating COWS and on gross energy intake and ether extract for non lactating cows.

Methane emissions from manure management were esti- mated using Tier 2 from IPCC (2006a). Organic matter digestibility of lactating cow diets was obtained in two ways: from the total tract collection of the in vivo experiment and from the equation of Heaney and Pigden (1963) based on the relationship between total digestible nutrient and organic matter digestibility. Total di- gestible nutrients were estimated from dietary Nel predicted by the model (Fox et al., 2004) using the following equation: TDN=(NEI + 0.12)/0.0245 (Moe and Tyrrell, 1976). The gross energy values needed to estimate volatile solid excretion rate were ob- tained by calorimetric measurement of the diets from in vivo experiment or using the equation of Ewan (1989).

Animal nitrogen excretion was determined as the nitrogen intake minus nitrogen output in milk minus the nitrogen stored in the animal body. Direct and indirect N20 emissions from manure storages were estimated as proposed by the Tier 2 method of IPCC (2006a). To compute the direct and indirect N20 emissions occurring during manure and synthetic nitrogen application on fields, Tier 1 of IPCC (2006b) methodology was applied. Carbon dioxide emissions from fuel combustion on farm were estimated according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emission factors and more details about IPCC equations adopted were reported by Bava et al. (2014).
An economic allocation was applied to milk and meat (preweaned male calves and cull cows sold) produced on the farm according to de Vries and de Boer (2010).
The environmental impact considered in this analysis was based on Global Warming Potential, which estimates the green- house gas emissions expressed as kg CO2 equivalent in a 100-year horizon.

The Life Cycle Assessment was carried out with the assistance of SimaPro 7.3.2 software (PRé Consultants, 2011). The impact assessment was performed with the EPD (2008) 1.03 method that uses the IPCC (2001) factors to convert CO2, CH4 and N20 into CO2 equivalent (respectively 1, 23 and 296).

1: Diet compositions are reported in Table 1. Diets were similar for starch and NDF concentrations; however, the diets were characterized by different forage DM percentages: 56.4 (CS), 51.6 (WPGS) and 42.9 (FS).

The experimental silages were characterized by ADL concentration: 2.9, 4.8 and 5.4% of DM for CS, WPGS and FS respectively. Lignin limits the rumen digestion of fiber and, as a consequence, rumen fill is increased and DMI can be reduced; this can affect milk yield and CH4 production. In the present study, there was a tendency (P = 0.07) for a lower DMI with FS than CS and WPGS

diets (Table 2) and cows fed the FS diet had a lower milk production 0.05) than cows fed the CS diet. The cows fed the WPGS diet provided intermediate results in terms of milk yield. Several studies (ex.: Ellis et al., 2009; Mills et al., 2003; Ramin and Huhtanen, 2013) showed that methane emission is positively related to DMI. In the present study, despite the trend for a different DMI, CH4 production (g/day) was not affected by diet (Table 2) although there was a positive trend between CH4 production (g/day) and DM, NDF and starch intakes (kg/day) (Fig. 2). In Fig. 2, the slope of the regression equation is higher for NDF intake than for starch intake. This means that for an increase of 1 kg of NDF or starch intake there is a concomitant increase in CH4 production of 20.0 g/day (P = 0.002) and 12.3 g/day 0.08), respectively.

Few studies (ex.: Benchaar et al., 2014; Brask et al., 2013; Doreau et al., 2013; Hassanat et al., 2013) investigated in vivo the effect of forage type (corn, barley or grass silages) on enteric CH4 emis- sions and in these studies, unlike ours, diets were not balanced for NDF and starch concentrations. Despite the similar carbohydrate con- tents, there were differences among diets when CH4 production was expressed as CH4 per kg of DMI (P = 0.09) or as CH4 per kg of NDF intake (P = 0.02).

The average production of CH4 (17.6 g/kg DMI) was similar to the value (16.3g/kg DMI) obtained by Beauchemin et al. (2009) for lac- tating primiparous COWS fed a control diet, and in the range reported by Hristov et al. (2013) (16-26 g/kg of DMI). Cows fed the FS diet had the highest methane production per kg of NDF intake (45.1 VS 48.1 VS 50.7 g/kg NDF intake, for CS, WPGS and FS, respectively; P = 0.02).

This difference could be explained by the different particle size of the diets which in turn affected passage rate and organic matter digest- ibility (the higher the particle size, the lower the passage rate). Particle size fraction retained on a 19 mm sieve was: 1.5, 4.8 and 18.5% for CS, WPGS and FS diets, respectively (Colombini et al., 2012). The in- fluence of passage rate on ruminal CH4 production was illustrated by Okine et al. (1989) who observed a significant reduction in CH4 production when weights were placed into the rumen of cattle to increase the passage rate. As a consequence, considering that the di- gestion rates of NDF were similar for the three silages (Colombini et al., 2012), and that the passage rate for the diet FS was presumably lower than those of the other diets, rumen NDF digestibility must have been higher for the FS diet; this was confirmed by the higher in vivo NDF digestibility (NDFD) of the FS diet in comparison with the other two diets (Table2 Fiber digestion enhances acetic acid production in the rumen and thereby CH4 production, and this resulted in a positive relationship between CH4 emission (g/kg DMI) and NDFD (%): CH4 (g/kg DMI) = 0.326 X NDFD + 4.13 r2=0.60).

On average, methane emission (g/kg of milk) was 13.9 without differences among diets although there was a tendency 0.06) for a higher emission with the FS diet when production was ex- pressed per kg of FPCM (Table 2). Overall, CH4 production per kg of milk was similar to the values obtained by Chung et al. (2012) for a control diet and lower than the values reported by Martin et al. (2008) and Lovett et al. (2005), probably due to the higher propor- tion (>65%) of forage in the diets used in their studies.

There was a significant difference among diets in terms of CH4 energy loss which was higher for the FS diet = 0.04). Methane energy losses averaged 5.37% of gross energy intake (GEI) which is very close to the average value of 5.72% obtained in a previous study that determined the effects of replacing alfalfa silage with corn silage in dairy cow diets (Hassanat et al., 2013). Similarly, Kebreab et al. (2008) used diet-specific mechanistic models to predict CH4 con- version rates for dairy cows, and they reported an average CH4 conversion rate of 5.63% of GEI. The diets CS and WPGS on average reduced CH4 energy loss by about 13% as compared to FS.

Significant linear and quadratic regressions were found between the percentage of CH4 energy loss on total GEI and the percentage of the dietary particles retained on a 19 mm sieve (Fig. 3). The results of the present study show that dietary particle size significantly affects CH4 energy loss. Cows fed the FS diet, with the higher frac- tion retained on a 19 mm sieve, had the highest CH4 energy loss as a percentage of total GEI.
To the best of our knowledge, the relationship between CH4 emission and diet particle size has not been evaluated in previous studies. However, Krause et al. (2002) showed that decreasing forage
Diet particle size >19 mm (%)
Fig. 3. Relationship between CH4 energy loss (%) and the diet percentage retained on a 19 mm sieve.

particle size increased the concentration of propionate in the diet; furthermore, in the same study, a lower dietary particle size decreased rumen pH. A lower rumen pH is less favorable for the growth of fibrolytic rumen bacteria which are known to be the main producers of CH4 in the rumen. Similarly the formation of propionate is a metabolic pathway which allows a lower CH4 production in comparison with the acetate metabolic pathway.

Methane energy losses averaged 5.37% of gross energy intake (GEI) which is very close to the average value of 5.72% obtained in a previous study that determined the effects of replacing alfalfa silage with corn silage in dairy cow diets (Hassanat et al., 2013). Similarly, Kebreab et al. (2008) used diet-specific mechanistic models to predict CH4 con- version rates for dairy cows, and they reported an average CH4 conversion rate of 5.63% of GEI. The diets CS and WPGS on average reduced CH4 energy loss by about 13% as compared to FS.

Significant linear and quadratic regressions were found between the percentage of CH4 energy loss on total GEI and the percentage of the dietary particles retained on a 19 mm sieve (Fig. 3). The results of the present study show that dietary particle size significantly affects CH4 energy loss. Cows fed the FS diet, with the higher frac- tion retained on a 19 mm sieve, had the highest CH4 energy loss as a percentage of total GEI.
To the best of our knowledge, the relationship between CH4 emission and diet particle size has not been evaluated in previous studies. However, Krause et al. (2002) showed that decreasing forage

particle size increased the concentration of propionate in the diet; furthermore, in the same study, a lower dietary particle size decreased rumen pH. A lower rumen pH is less favorable for the growth of fibrolytic rumen bacteria which are known to be the main producers of CH4 in the rumen. Similarly the formation of propionate is a metabolic pathway which allows a lower CH4 production in comparison with the acetate metabolic pathway.
11: <header>3.2. LCA analysis of three forage systems </header>

As expected CH4 emission from enteric fermentation and manure storage was the major contributor to GHG emission of milk pro- duction: the average percentage between the three scenarios accounted for 45.4% of the total GHG. On average, 71.1% of CH4 emitted resulted from enteric production.

Predicted enteric methane emissions from lactating cows were generally lower than in vivo measurements with the exception of the CS diet which had similar predicted and in vivo values. The estimated CH4 daily production from lactating cows was 323 g/day per cow (average of the three diets) and the in vivo value was 340 g/day, both similar to the results obtained by Grainger et al. (2007). The most important differences between predicted and measured emissions were observed for the FS and WPGS diets where in vivo enteric methane was 11% and 4% higher than the estimated one, while for the CS diet the difference was only 0.2%. This could be ascribed on one side to the fact that most equations to predict CH4 emission were derived from in vivo studies utilizing diets based on corn rather than sorghum silages; on the other side, the passage rate, not considered by any equation, could have affected the emissions, especially for the FS diet.

Considering enteric methane emissions from lactating cows ex- pressed as CH4 per kg of FPCM, the in vivo experiment produced an average value of the three diets of 13.6 g CH4/kg FPCM while the software SIMAPRO estimated 21.2 CH4/kg FPCM. The difference is mostly explained by the fact that the software calculated the CH4 production of the whole herd, including the non-producing animals (heifers and dry cows). This result underlines the high contribu- tion of non-producing animals, especially replacement heifers, to the global warming potential of milk production. According to Place and Mitloehner (2010), efficient heifer management can improve milk's life-cycle production efficiency and decrease emissions.

Table 3 reports CH4 emissions (expressed as kg CO2 eq. per kg FPCM) in the three forage system scenarios calculated both from measured and predicted enteric methane. Among the three sce- narios, CS had the lowest CH4 emissions per milk unit, particularly when estimated enteric methane was used in the calculation.

The percentage of methane emissions from enteric fermenta- tion and manure storage changed very little among diets.

Fig. 4 shows the GWP of milk production in the three scenarios using both measured or predicted CH4; these results were slightly higher than findings obtained by other authors for milk produc- tion in intensive dairy farms (Fantin et al., 2012; Guerci et al., 2012) probably due to the lower milk production observed in this study. No important differences were noticed among the three sce- narios; the GWP obtained using measured methane had slightly higher values compared to estimated methane scenarios.

Corn silage produced the lowest global warming potential per kg of FPCM. Belflower et al. (2012) found a possible reduction of 15% of GWP including more corn silage and grain in the dairy cow diet in a pasture-based system. Nguyen et al. (2013), who evaluated the opportunity of switching from corn silage-based to grass-based dairy systems, concluded that corn silage-based systems can reduce the global warming potential per kg of milk produced. Corn silage is a high energy forage and in the North of Italy it is mainly produced on farms; as a consequence the inclusion of corn silage in the dairy cow diet allows a decrease in purchased feed, increases farm feed
self-sufficiency, and sustains high milk production (Guerci et al., 2013a).
Table 4 shows the contributions of inputs and activities to GWP in the three scenarios.

Enteric and animal housing contributions were similar using in vivo enteric CH4 and in predicted CH4 data. Concentrate feed pur- chased had a great load on global warming potential (29.6% on average); the highest percentage was in the FS scenario, because the diet of dairy cows had to be integrated with more off-farm corn meal than in the CS and WPGS scenarios. On the contrary, the con- tribution of crop production on farm to global warming potential was lower for the sorghum scenarios, in particular FS, in compar- ison to the CS scenario because of the reduced use of water and fertilizers in sorghum production.
